Case study · Strategy

Featured case studies

Three campaigns picked to show range: automation depth and multi-brand complexity, DTC vs. B2B lifecycle thinking, and adapting a distinct brand voice into clear, high-converting campaigns.

3 featured · tap to expand
OnlyBlooms.ca Real client Lifecycle CRM and campaign system for a Canadian curated marketplace Lifecycle strategyKlaviyoShopify 2026
Lifecycle strategyKlaviyoShopifyHubSpot CRMMarketplaceCampaign production

A real lifecycle and email-marketing project for OnlyBlooms.ca, a Canadian curated marketplace carrying 1,000+ products across beauty, wellness, gourmet, home, gifting, and everyday essentials. My role: turn a large, constantly changing catalogue into a structured CRM system — automated journeys, category-based campaigns, and a repeatable production process connecting strategy, copy, design, technical implementation, and reporting. Unlike the case studies below, the campaigns, flows, offers, and figures here come from real work.

The challenge

OnlyBlooms' catalogue spans very different buying behaviors — coffee suits replenishment, skincare needs education, gourmet products pair with recipes and occasions, gift sets are occasion-driven. One universal newsletter couldn't serve all of it. The CRM needed to onboard new subscribers, convert first-order discount holders, recover carts without defaulting to another discount, support individual brand promotions, and encourage cross-category discovery — all in mobile-first creative at a high campaign volume.

The strategic decision

I treated OnlyBlooms as a curated discovery marketplace, not one undifferentiated store: one customer profile, multiple category and brand affinities. A Kosterina olive-oil shopper shouldn't see the same content as a Fygg toothpaste or AZORÉE LAB skincare shopper — while every campaign still had to feel recognisably OnlyBlooms through consistent sender identity, shared header/footer systems, and mobile-first layout.

What I worked on
Lifecycle strategy and customer-journey planning
Campaign-calendar development
Segmentation and flow architecture
Subject lines, preview text, sender-name systems
Campaign and automation copy
Banner messaging and visual direction
Shopify and website-offer verification
Klaviyo implementation support
Mobile/desktop QA and link, price, discount validation
Campaign reporting and optimisation
Under the hood

The infrastructure behind the campaigns above — not just the creative. Three artifacts from the build: an automation flow, the conditional logic that routes customers between flows, and the production pipeline every campaign moved through before sending.

Klaviyo · abandoned-cart-flow Flow map
TriggerCart created, checkout not completed
WaitA few hours, no purchase?
Email 1Reminder, voiced as "Maya from Customer Care"
WaitNext day, still no purchase?
Email 2Final nudge, no discount by default
Result16% recovery rate

Purchasing at any point exits the flow immediately — one of the three automation flows built from zero in ~2 months.

Klaviyo · flow-exit-logic Conditional logic
Ifcustomer redeems the welcome discount→ exitsthe unused-discount reminder flow
Ifcustomer completes checkout→ exitsthe abandoned-cart flow immediately
Iforder has an unresolved fulfilment issue→ routes tocustomer care, ahead of any promotional send
Ifcustomer clicks a category in the welcome series→ tags intothat category's ongoing campaign segment
Production tracker · campaign-pipeline Workflow
01Offer verification against live site
02Audience definition & exclusions
03Message hierarchy
04Copy system
05Visual direction
06QA: links, prices, mobile/desktop

Every campaign moved through all six steps before sending — same pipeline regardless of which of the 15+ brands it was for.

Lifecycle map

Visitor → Subscriber → Discount available → Product explorer → Cart created → Checkout started → First-time buyer → Delivered customer → Second-time buyer → Repeat customer → Loyal customer → At risk → Lapsed. Customers moved on behavior, not a fixed schedule — using the welcome discount exited the unused-discount flow, purchasing exited cart recovery, an unresolved order issue bumped the customer to customer care ahead of any promotion.

Automation system

Built and optimised six core automation flows from zero in about two months: a category-led welcome journey (Shop All → Beauty & Wellness → Gourmet Rituals → Kitchen & Home → Gift Ideas → Brand Spotlight → Bestsellers), an unused-discount reminder, a three-email abandoned-cart flow voiced as "Maya from OnlyBlooms Customer Care," routine-based upsell/cross-sell logic (toothpaste → floss; coffee → brewing gear; serum → cleanser), a cashback loyalty journey, and post-purchase/reactivation automation.

Selected real campaigns
Kosterina sale + giftBuy-two-get-a-gift Mediterranean pantry offer, without letting "free" or the gift overshadow the products.
Oral-care categoryAny two oral-care products + a Davids floss gift; sender adapted to "Only Blooms | Oral Care" for instant recognition.
AZORÉE LAB back-in-stockOne product, one CTA — built as a reusable back-in-stock framework, not a catalogue dump.
Canada Day multi-offerFive simultaneous brand offers (Purity Coffee, Nudge, AZORÉE LAB, La Sultane de Saba, Melinda's) under one clear visual hierarchy.
Purity CoffeeProduct education plus a qualifying-purchase offer — coffee positioned as a ritual, not a grid item.
Rideau Florist partnership$20-off acquisition campaign introducing OnlyBlooms to an existing florist customer base.
Production system
  • 01Offer verification — confirm eligible products, quantity, gift availability, dates, and discount stacking against the live site.
  • 02Audience definition — who receives it, who's excluded, category-specific or previous-purchaser logic.
  • 03Message hierarchy — what's the offer, what qualifies, what do they get, when does it end, what do they click.
  • 04Copy system — internal name, sender, subject, preview text, hero, product copy, CTA, footer.
  • 05Visual direction — brand colors/type, image hierarchy, mobile readability, CTA consistency.
  • 06QA — links, prices, gift conditions, sender info, mobile/desktop rendering, unsubscribe functionality.
Tools
Klaviyo Shopify HubSpot CRM Canva Figma Stripo Claude Design Custom HTML Google Sheets reporting
Results
Automation system built from zero in approximately two months
52%Average open rate across six automation flows built during the first two months
18%Average click-through rate across the newly built automation system
16%Abandoned-cart recovery rate generated by the cart-recovery journey
24%Coupon-redemption rate among eligible recipients

My contribution: Lifecycle strategy, flow architecture, segmentation logic, copy, design direction, implementation coordination, QA and performance reporting.

OnlyBlooms is a real client project. The company, products, and public promotional examples are real. The 52% and 18% figures are averages across the six-flow automation system; the 16% and 24% figures are specific to the cart-recovery journey and coupon offers respectively — all percentages come from internal lifecycle reporting used in my professional portfolio. Revenue figures, customer counts, list sizes, and other commercially sensitive account data are not disclosed.

Jane & Bloom Real client DTC lifecycle and B2B wholesale CRM strategy for a spa-gifting brand B2B wholesaleKlaviyoFaire 2026
KlaviyoShopifyFaireWholesaleRetailer acquisition

A real email-marketing and lifecycle-strategy project for Jane & Bloom, a Canadian spa-gifting brand — curated bath, body, and relaxation gift sets for personal, seasonal, corporate, and retail gifting. Unlike the five case studies above, this one covers two connected customer journeys at once: DTC shoppers buying through the Shopify store, and independent retailers buying wholesale through Faire. The core challenge was communicating the same products differently depending on who was buying them.

DTC vs. B2B value proposition
DTC customer asksWill she like it? Is it ready to give? Will it arrive on time? → focus on emotion, occasion, presentation.
Retailer asksWill it sell? What margin? Is it shelf-ready? How fast can I reorder? → focus on retail fit, margin, merchandising.
Same product, two storiesThe Royal Retreat box is "a private spa escape" to a customer, and "a premium, ready-to-sell gift box with broad appeal" to a retailer.
Lifecycle maps

DTC: Visitor → Subscriber → Gift explorer → Occasion identified → First-time buyer → Repeat gift buyer → Loyal customer → At risk → Lapsed. B2B: Retailer discovered brand → Sample considered → Opening order → Products merchandised → Reorder → Seasonal buyer → High-value retailer → Dormant. A retailer doesn't stay in generic acquisition messaging after their opening order — they move straight into sell-through support.

What I worked on
B2C and B2B wholesale campaign strategy
Faire campaign planning and retailer segmentation
Wholesale-margin and MSRP communication
Product-bundle development and retailer-use-case messaging
Shopify and Faire product research, website-to-email validation
Lifecycle-flow design for both channels
Reorder and reactivation logic
Campaign-calendar planning across DTC and B2B
Under the hood

Two customer types, two sets of infrastructure. The B2B onboarding flow, the suppression logic keeping DTC and wholesale audiences from crossing wires, and the two lifecycle tracks running in parallel.

Klaviyo · sample-to-opening-order Flow map
TriggerWholesale sample delivered
Email 1Delivery confirmation
Email 2Merchandising guidance
Email 3Margin explanation
ConditionOpening order placed?
ResultOpening order → retailer onboarding flow

The most important B2B journey — retailer goes straight into sell-through support after ordering, not generic acquisition messaging.

Klaviyo · dtc-b2b-suppression-rules Conditional logic
Ifretailer already ordered during Faire Market→ exitsthe event campaign sequence
Ifcontact is a sample buyer→ enterswholesale conversion, not consumer welcome messaging
Ifretailer has an unresolved fulfilment issue→ suppressed fromreorder-request sends
Ifcontact is tagged DTC or B2B→ never receivesthe other audience's pricing
Ifcustomer bought a birthday gift→ exitsbirthday-reminder flow immediately
Lifecycle map · dtc-vs-b2b Dual funnel
DTCVisitor
Gift explorer
First-time buyer
Repeat gift buyer
Loyal → at risk → lapsed
B2BRetailer discovers brand
Sample considered
Opening order
Reorder → seasonal buyer
High-value → dormant

Same brand, two parallel lifecycle tracks — a retailer doesn't stay in generic acquisition messaging after their opening order.

Selected real B2B campaign: Faire Market 2026

A wholesale campaign targeting existing and previous retailers ahead of Faire Market (July 21–23, 2026, early access July 20, up to 30% savings). It sold a single low-complexity decision — a summer-only three-set bestseller bundle at CAD $80 wholesale / CAD $179 combined MSRP / ~55% retailer margin — instead of asking retailers to evaluate the whole catalogue. I built it as a four-part sequence: market preview → "Faire Market is live" → mid-event margin spotlight (aimed at openers who hadn't ordered) → final-day reminder, suppressed the moment a qualifying order came in.

B2B Faire flows
  • 01New wholesale-retailer welcome — brand proposition, bestsellers, store fit, sample options, opening-order guidance.
  • 02Sample-to-opening-order — the most important B2B journey: delivery confirmation → merchandising guidance → margin explanation → opening-order CTA.
  • 03Opening-order onboarding — not just inventory delivery, but helping the retailer actually sell it: display ideas, suggested pricing, restock guidance.
  • 04First sell-through support — display examples, social captions, staff talking points, timed to when the retailer has actually merchandised the product.
  • 05Reorder reminder — "Running low on Royal Retreat? Reorder before your next gifting weekend" — tied to estimated reorder cycle, not a fixed schedule.
  • 06Dormant-retailer reactivation — a commercially relevant reason to return first; a discount is not the automatic opener.
DTC lifecycle flows

Welcome & gift discovery ("Who are you shopping for?"), occasion-based browse recovery, cart recovery without an automatic discount, birthday/anniversary reminders (21 / 10 / 5 / 1–2 days out), repeat-gifting for annual occasions, and a dedicated corporate-gifting journey for business buyers.

Segmentation
Wholesale prospect Sample buyer Opening-order buyer High-value retailer Dormant retailer Gift shop / spa / boutique DTC occasion affinity Corporate-gifting interest Canada / US
Communication-priority logic
  • A retailer who already ordered during Faire Market exits the event sequence
  • A sample buyer enters wholesale conversion, not consumer welcome messaging
  • A retailer with an unresolved fulfilment issue doesn't get a reorder request
  • DTC and B2B audiences must never receive one another's pricing
  • A customer who bought a birthday gift exits birthday reminders immediately
Tools
Klaviyo Shopify Faire HubSpot CRM Canva Figma
Outcomes

Separate DTC and B2B value propositions; a dedicated, reusable Faire event sequence; visible retailer margin and MSRP in every wholesale send; sample-to-opening-order lifecycle logic; and a scalable structure for seasonal retail buying campaigns.

Jane & Bloom is a real client project. The brand, website, products, Faire presence, and Faire Market campaign are real; the bundle, B2B banner structure, and retailer campaign work are based on real project work. The DTC and B2B lifecycle-flow sections show the broader CRM architecture designed around the project — flows not yet fully launched are shown as designed systems, not measured implementations. No wholesale revenue or Faire conversion figures are included, as I only present results directly attributable and verifiable through this account's own reporting.

La Sultane de Saba Canada Real client Campaign and lifecycle CRM strategy for a luxury beauty ritual brand Luxury beautyKlaviyoCampaign strategy 2026
KlaviyoShopifyLuxury beautyFragranceEmail design

A real email-marketing and CRM project for La Sultane de Saba Canada, a luxury beauty retailer offering French-made body care, fragrances, and gift sets inspired by traditional beauty rituals — fulfilled locally in Canada under OnlyBlooms Import Inc. My work translated the brand's sensorial identity into commercially clear campaigns: seasonal rituals, launches, restocks, gift sets, tiered rewards, and product education. The core challenge was keeping the brand's poetry intact while making the offer, conditions, and next action clear within seconds.

The brand

Unlike a store organised by skin concern, the catalogue is structured around two dimensions: product type (body lotion, shower cream, scrubs, fragrance, gift sets) and a "scent journey" — 12 named destinations like Journey to Bali, the Spices Route, and Rituals of Hammam, each tying several products to one sensorial world. That's an unusual CRM opportunity: customers can be segmented by both product used and journey preferred.

The challenge

Luxury copy has to create desire; e-commerce copy has to create clarity. Long poetic product names, near-identical packaging, and gift conditions competing with storytelling all fight for very little mobile space. The goal wasn't to strip out the brand's emotional language — it was to put that language inside a hierarchy where the customer still instantly understood what's being shown, why now, what's included, and what to click. Working principle: emotion creates desire, structure creates conversion.

What I worked on
Campaign strategy and promotional-offer analysis
Subject lines, preview text, hero and banner copy
Brand tone-of-voice adaptation
Email content hierarchy, mobile-first copy
Visual direction for banners
Gift-condition communication
Product and offer verification
Campaign QA and lifecycle recommendations
Under the hood

The structure behind the sensorial copy: the recommended automation architecture, the segmentation logic driving it, and the four-layer message framework every campaign was built on.

Klaviyo · lifecycle-architecture Recommended, not yet launched
01Welcome & journey discovery
02Browse & cart recovery
03First-purchase onboarding
04Ritual completion
05Replenishment & back-in-stock
06High-value & reactivation

My delivered work was campaign strategy and production; this automation layer is designed architecture, shown honestly as recommended — not a claim every flow has launched.

Klaviyo · segment-tagging-rules Segmentation logic
Ifcustomer engages with "Journey to Bali" content→ tagsJourney to Bali affinity, prioritized for that journey's sends
Ifcustomer purchases a gift set→ tagsgift-set buyer, enters gift-responsive segment
Ifcustomer crosses the free-shipping threshold→ tagsfree-shipping-threshold customer
Ifcustomer goes quiet past the lapsed window→ tagslapsed, selective non-automatic reactivation only
Campaign brief · message-framework Briefing structure
Layer 1Emotional destination — e.g. a journey to Bali
Layer 2Practical product story — cleanse → exfoliate → hydrate
Layer 3Commercial reason to act — restock, tiered gift, deadline
Layer 4Direct next action — "Shop the Ritual"

The brief every designer and I worked from — keeps the copy poetic while the CTA stays unambiguous.

Campaign framework

Every campaign was built around four layers: an emotional destination (a journey to Bali, a summer ritual), a practical product story (cleanse → exfoliate → hydrate → illuminate), a commercial reason to act (restock, tiered gift, deadline), and a direct next action ("Shop the Ritual"). That let the copy stay poetic while the CTA stayed unambiguous.

Selected real campaigns
Summer Skin RitualA 3-step body ritual (balm → lotion → glow) reframed as a sequence, not an unrelated product grid.
Care Pouch gift setLimited-edition set at $118 vs. $128 regular — price gap made immediately visible, packaging kept accurate.
Journey to Bali promo10% off + $200 threshold + guaranteed gift + mystery-gift entry, ordered so the guaranteed gift was never confused with the prize.
Tiered beauty giftsFour reward tiers ($50–$300+) made scannable without turning the email into a pricing table.
Ayurvedic Body Lotion restockOne product, one scent story, one CTA — no unrelated cross-sells diluting the announcement.
Multi-brand gift-with-purchaseSame brand aesthetic adapted to work inside a wider OnlyBlooms retail campaign.
Segmentation strategy
Journey to Bali affinity Spices Route affinity Body lotion buyers Perfume buyers Gift-set buyers Gift-responsive customers Free-shipping-threshold customers First-time buyer High-value customer Lapsed customer
Lifecycle journeys (recommended extension)
  • 01Welcome & journey discovery — a scent-preference question ("Where should your senses travel first?") informs future recommendations.
  • 02Browse & cart recovery — the exact journey explored, with no aggressive urgency for a luxury-consideration purchase.
  • 03First-purchase onboarding — usage guidance and fragrance layering, not an immediate upsell.
  • 04Ritual completion — one product from a journey without its complementary steps (shower cream → body lotion → perfume).
  • 05Replenishment & back-in-stock — timed to product size and repurchase behavior; restock uses the one-product, one-CTA format.
  • 06High-value & reactivation — early access and tiered gifts for top customers; selective, non-automatic discounting for lapsed ones.

My delivered work concentrated on campaign strategy and production; this automation layer is presented as recommended architecture, not a claim that every flow has already launched.

Mobile-first design principles
  • One primary headline, one dominant offer per section
  • No copy placed over important packaging
  • Order thresholds sized to stay readable without zooming
  • Product dominates the composition for restocks
  • Ritual sequences stay clear even stacked vertically
  • Product accuracy prioritized over decorative effects
Tools
Klaviyo Shopify Canva Figma Campaign planning docs AI-assisted copy & design ideation
Outcomes

A clearer luxury tone-of-voice framework; stronger separation between emotional and commercial copy; understandable tiered-gift communication; a reusable focused back-in-stock format; more consistent subject/preview pairing; and clearer visual briefs for designers.

La Sultane de Saba Canada is a real client project. The brand, products, promotions, gift structures, campaign concepts, and email assets described here are based on my real work; public assortment and fulfilment details were verified against the brand's website. No standalone revenue or conversion figures are included, as I only present metrics directly attributable and verifiable through client reporting.

Purity Coffee in Canada by Only Gourmet Real client Lifecycle CRM and campaign strategy for a coffee and brewing retailer CoffeeKlaviyoReplenishment
KlaviyoShopifyCoffeeProduct educationCross-sell

A real email-marketing project for Purity Coffee in Canada by Only Gourmet, an Ottawa-based retailer selling organic, specialty-grade coffee alongside brewing equipment, storage products, and Apolis Tea. My work turned a technically differentiated product — five roast profiles, six formats, third-party mold and mycotoxin testing — into commercially clear campaigns: helping customers choose a roast and format, running gift-with-purchase offers, promoting equipment, converting interested non-buyers, and building reasons to come back.

The challenge

The brand's advantages — five named roasts (FLOW, PROTECT, EASE, CALM, BALANCE), six formats from whole bean to instant, a 2,268g large-bag option, and real replenishment potential — also meant a new visitor could easily get lost. They might not know the difference between roasts, whether they need whole bean or ground, or why a bulk bag matters. The email strategy had to do more than announce discounts — it had to help the customer make a confident product decision. Working principle: education builds confidence, a clear offer creates action, replenishment creates retention.

What I worked on
Campaign strategy and promotional-offer development
Audience definition and roast/format positioning
Subject lines, preview text, hero and banner copy
Gift-with-purchase communication
Mobile-first content hierarchy
Visual direction and product/pricing QA
Website and email consistency
Lifecycle and retention recommendations
Selected real campaigns
Father's Day gifts10% off + tiered rewards, built around a BruTrek mug, coffee, and Apolis Tea — practical gifting routes, not a generic holiday discount.
PURE10 non-buyer conversionTargeted engaged browsers who'd never ordered — moved quickly from interest to decision rather than a broad welcome flow.
Buy two, get an Adventure MugEncouraged trying two roasts or combining regular/decaf, raising order value without a straight percentage discount.
Large-bag (2,268g) campaignFramed as fewer reorders and a consistent supply for frequent drinkers — not a bulk/industrial pitch.
Fellow Stagg EKG kettleTechnical specs (gooseneck, temp control) broken into short visual banners instead of dense paragraphs.
Cold brew & discovery bundleSeasonal ritual framing for cold brew; a 5-roast bundle positioned for discovery and gifting.
Segmentation strategy
FLOW / PROTECT / EASE buyers CALM / BALANCE (decaf) buyers Whole bean vs. ground vs. pods Large-bag buyers Accessory & equipment buyers Subscriber, never purchased Repeat coffee buyer Tea buyer Discount-responsive buyer Lapsed buyer
Lifecycle programme (recommended extension)
  • 01Welcome & coffee finder — "What does your ideal cup look like?" determines the first recommendation instead of showing every product.
  • 02Interested non-buyer conversion — extends the PURE10 logic: differentiation → roast guide → reviews → offer → final reminder.
  • 03Browse & cart recovery — content branches by what was viewed (CALM browser gets decaf info, kettle browser gets specs, not coffee messaging).
  • 04First-order education — storage, grinding, and brew-ratio guidance tailored to the format purchased.
  • 05Replenishment & roast exploration — timed to pack size and consumption; loyal single-roast buyers get a controlled introduction to another roast.
  • 06Format expansion & equipment cross-sell — whole-bean buyer → sachets for travel; repeat buyer with no grinder → grinder recommendation.
  • 07Large-bag upgrade & reactivation — offered only where consumption history supports it; lapsed messaging leads with relevance before discounting.

My delivered work concentrated on campaign strategy and production; this automation layer is a recommended lifecycle extension, not a claim that every flow has already launched.

Health-positioned copy & compliance
  • Use only claims supported and approved by the brand
  • Distinguish product testing from medical outcomes
  • Never imply the product diagnoses, treats, or prevents disease
  • Avoid turning customer testimonials into universal health promises
  • Keep taste, roast, origin, and preparation central to the copy
  • Stay consistent with product pages and legal disclaimers
Tools
Klaviyo Shopify Canva Figma Claude Campaign planning docs Reporting dashboards
Outcomes

A stronger distinction between roast-led and format-led campaigns; reusable gift-with-purchase and large-bag structures; a repeatable interested-non-buyer format; shorter, more visual equipment education; and a clearer separation between product claims and promotional language.

Purity Coffee in Canada by Only Gourmet is a real client project. The campaigns described — Father's Day, PURE10, the Adventure Mug gift, the large-bag promotion, the Fellow Stagg EKG campaign, and cold-brew communication — are based on real work. Public assortment, format, pricing, and shipping details were checked against the live storefront. I would not add revenue, conversion, or open/click-rate figures unless they can be isolated to this account and verified in its reporting.

OnlyGourmet.ca Real client Campaign and lifecycle CRM system for a multi-brand gourmet retailer Multi-brandKlaviyo32 campaigns
KlaviyoShopifyHubSpot CRMGourmet retailSegmentation

A real email-marketing and CRM project for OnlyGourmet.ca, a Canadian multi-brand retailer and distribution business — Kosterina, Purity Coffee, Apolis Tea, Fellow, Ketone-IQ, Collins, and more, all sold through one storefront. My role was to translate that diverse catalogue into structured, commercially clear campaigns while letting each brand keep its own visual and verbal identity. I worked across 32 email campaigns for OnlyGourmet and its brand portfolio.

The challenge

This wasn't one brand with one customer need — it was replenishable products (coffee, tea, oil, protein), considered equipment (kettles, grinders), discovery-led items (new tea flavors, wellness products), and gift-led bundles, all in one database. A universal send strategy meant coffee buyers seeing energy-shot promos, a premium Kosterina campaign looking identical to a functional Ketone-IQ one, and technical Fellow products getting too little explanation. I treated OnlyGourmet as a portfolio of customer rituals — morning coffee, tea and relaxation, Mediterranean cooking, home brewing, functional energy — with one retailer identity, distinct brand worlds, and one clear action per campaign.

What I worked on
Campaign strategy and calendar planning across the brand portfolio
Audience and segment definition by category and brand
Sender-name development and brand tone-of-voice adaptation
Offer analysis and gift-condition validation
Website-to-email consistency checks
Klaviyo implementation support and campaign QA
Cross-functional coordination with design and technical teams
Campaign reporting
Brand architecture

Every campaign carried two layers of identity: a consistent retailer layer (sender recognition, shipping/returns, shared structure, footer) and a brand layer that shifted with the product — Kosterina Mediterranean and premium, Purity Coffee educational and routine-based, Apolis Tea craft and discovery-led, Fellow minimalist and design-led, Ketone-IQ direct and benefit-led, Collins social and occasion-led. A customer should recognize an OnlyGourmet email without every brand campaign looking or sounding the same.

Selected real campaigns
Kosterina sale + giftBuy-two-get-a-vinegar offer stacked with a collection sale, without letting the gift outshine the qualifying products.
Apolis Tea + brewer giftTwo teas + a glass brewer, framed as one tea ritual rather than two unrelated products.
Purity Coffee PURE10 + BruTrek giftNon-buyer conversion and a two-for-a-mug offer — same real campaigns as the standalone Purity Coffee case.
Father's Day multi-categoryOne occasion, four product routes (coffee, tea, travel mug, brewing gear) instead of one stereotypical gift.
Fellow Stagg EKG educationA technical kettle broken into five short visual ideas instead of one spec sheet.
Cold brew & large-bagSeasonal ritual framing for cold brew; fewer-reorders framing for the bulk format.
Segmentation strategy
Coffee / tea / pantry affinity Kosterina / Purity Coffee / Apolis / Fellow loyalists Replenishment buyer Equipment buyer Gift buyer Cross-category buyer Single-brand loyalist Sale-responsive buyer High-value customer Lapsed customer
Website & campaign QA

A live-site audit surfaced a real risk: the homepage advertised free delivery over $50 while the shipping policy said $39.99, and the About page claimed US delivery while the shipping policy said Canada only. Inconsistencies like that create customer confusion, support volume, and failed campaign QA — so every send was checked against the live purchase journey rather than a reused text block.

Health & product-claim control
  • Use only approved product claims, matched to verified product pages
  • Distinguish product features from medical outcomes
  • Avoid diagnosis, prevention, treatment, or cure language
  • Avoid turning testimonials into universal claims
  • Clearly identify caffeine content where relevant
  • Review regulated nutrition or wellness language before launch
Recommended lifecycle extension
  • 01Welcome & preference discovery — coffee, tea, pantry, energy, or cooking — determines the featured products from message one.
  • 02First-purchase conversion — branches on known brand interest, known category interest, or no browsing history at all.
  • 03Browse & cart recovery — a Fellow viewer gets specs, a Kosterina viewer gets recipes; discounting isn't the automatic first move.
  • 04First-order onboarding & replenishment — storage/prep guidance by product, timed replenishment for coffee, tea, oil, and protein.
  • 05Routine & brand-to-brand cross-sell — coffee → kettle; tea → glass brewer; Purity Coffee buyer → Fellow equipment, with a stated reason for the connection.
  • 06Back-in-stock & at-risk recovery — one product, one reason, one CTA; relevance before discounting for lapsed customers.

My delivered work concentrated on campaign strategy and production; this lifecycle layer is a recommended extension, not a claim that every flow has already launched.

Tools
Klaviyo Shopify HubSpot CRM Canva Figma Claude Reporting dashboards
Outcomes

A clearer multi-brand campaign system; reusable gift-with-purchase structures; stronger separation between brand and retailer identity; better category and brand segmentation; more systematic offer and website-to-email QA; and clearer designer briefs across a 32-campaign workload.

OnlyGourmet is a real client project. The company, brand portfolio, product categories, campaigns, and promotional structures described are based on real work; public catalogue, fulfilment, and shipping details were checked against the live website. I'm not including isolated revenue, open-rate, click-rate, or conversion figures, as I only present metrics directly attributable and verifiable through the account's own reporting.

Rideau Florist Ottawa Real client Email, SMS, and occasion-reminder CRM for a local flower-delivery business Birthday remindersKlaviyoSMS marketing
KlaviyoShopifyEmail marketingSMS marketingLocal e-commerce

A real lifecycle-marketing project for Rideau Florist, a local Ottawa flower and gifting business offering bouquets, plants, spa gifts, and occasion-based delivery with same-day service across Ottawa, the Ottawa Valley, and Gatineau. Unlike the retail case studies above, this one isn't about catalogue complexity — it's about remembering who the gift is for and when they need it again.

The commercial opportunity

Flower purchasing is predictable but infrequent — a customer might not order for eight months and still not be disengaged, because they're simply waiting for the next birthday or anniversary. A standard inactivity model would misclassify these customers as lapsed. A date-based model instead asks who the gift was for, what the occasion was, and when it recurs — then uses email and SMS to help the customer act before the date passes.

The challenge

The purchaser and recipient are different people, so ordinary customer data is incomplete without recipient name, relationship, and occasion date attached to it. Demand is highly seasonal (Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, graduation), orders are emotionally high-stakes — a late delivery isn't just an e-commerce inconvenience, it can be a missed birthday or funeral — and same-day delivery creates real urgency that has to be handled with carefully timed SMS rather than blanket promotional sends.

The strategic decision

I structured the programme around an occasion calendar, not just order frequency: Visitor → Subscriber → Occasion identified → Reminder scheduled → Product explorer → Order placed → Gift delivered → Recipient occasion saved → Next reminder cycle. Working principle: remember the person, remember the date, reduce the effort required to send something meaningful.

What I worked on
Lifecycle strategy across email and SMS
Birthday-reminder architecture and occasion segmentation
Automated flow structure across purchaser and recipient data
Seasonal calendar planning and audience exclusions
Product and delivery verification
Shopify and Klaviyo coordination
Cross-brand campaign communication
Post-purchase and repeat-order logic
Email vs. SMS strategy
EmailInspiration, comparison, and product discovery — gift guides, birthday/anniversary collections, flower-care education.
SMSShort, time-sensitive, consent-based only — "birthday's next week," "same-day still available," never a duplicate of every email.
Consent & frequencyOne promotional SMS per window max; birthday/anniversary reminders prioritized over generic sales; immediate suppression on opt-out.
Birthday-reminder programme
  • 0121 days before — email, early inspiration and broad selection while there's still time to plan.
  • 0210 days before — personalized birthday collection, referencing the previous gift and budget.
  • 035 days before — email or SMS depending on consent; increases urgency and confirms delivery area.
  • 041 day before — SMS only, last practical reminder, sent only when delivery can still realistically happen.

The flow exits the moment an order completes, and the birthday auto-resets for the following year unless the customer removes it. Anniversary reminders reuse the same infrastructure with different merchandising (romantic bouquets, roses, spa gifts) and a longer lead time.

Automated flows
  • 05Browse & cart recovery — a sympathy browser gets a restrained, supportive tone; a birthday browser gets standard urgency.
  • 06Checkout & delivery-date recovery — more urgent than ordinary cart abandonment, since a real occasion date may be approaching and the slot isn't reserved until purchase.
  • 07Same-day delivery flow — activates only when operational capacity allows; one CTA, one cut-off time, no catalogue browsing.
  • 08Order & delivery communication — confirmed, in preparation, out for delivery, delivered — always prioritized over promotions.
  • 09Post-purchase flower care & satisfaction — care guidance first, review request delayed until any service issue closes.
  • 10Second-occasion discovery — one relevant next reminder after delivery (birthday buyer → save another birthday), not a full occasion list at once.
Selected real campaign

Rideau Florist × OnlyBlooms introduction — a $20-off cross-brand campaign (code RIDEAU20) introducing OnlyBlooms to the existing Rideau Florist audience. The structure explained the connection between the businesses first, then the offer, positioning it as an extension of gifting rather than an unrelated ad: flowers for the occasion → gifts and lifestyle products for future needs.

Segmentation
Birthday / anniversary affinity Sympathy customer Business-gifting customer Same-day buyer Early planner Last-minute buyer Annual reminder customer Add-on purchaser Email-only / SMS-only / both Lapsed customer
Communication-priority logic
  • Order, payment, and delivery communication always come first
  • A delivery problem suppresses all promotional SMS
  • A completed order exits the birthday reminder flow immediately
  • Sympathy customers are excluded from light-hearted urgency messaging
  • Business customers get professional gifting language, not romantic copy
  • Same-day messages stop the moment operational capacity closes
Tools
Klaviyo Shopify Email automation SMS automation Canva Figma
Outcomes

Email and SMS working as coordinated channels rather than duplicates; annual reminder logic replacing generic inactivity messaging; a clearer distinction between purchaser and recipient data; more sensitive suppression around service issues and sympathy orders; and a structured cross-brand acquisition campaign built on top of an existing local audience.

Rideau Florist is a real client project. The catalogue, local delivery model, gifting categories, birthday-reminder work, SMS planning, and cross-brand campaign described are based on real client work; public product, delivery, and policy details were checked against the live website. Birthday, anniversary, and SMS automation sections describe the lifecycle architecture developed for the project — any flow not yet fully launched is shown as designed system, not a measured implementation. I'm not including open-rate, click-rate, SMS conversion, or revenue figures, as I only present metrics directly attributable and verifiable through this account's own reporting.

AZORÉE LAB Real client Lifecycle CRM and product-education strategy for a clean skincare brand SkincareKlaviyoSubscriptions
KlaviyoShopifyProduct educationRoutine buildingReplenishment

A real email-marketing project for AZORÉE LAB, a Canadian clean-skincare brand combining minimalist formulations, plant-based ingredients, and modern actives (Bakuchiol, copper peptides, Hyaluronic Acid, Niacinamide, Vitamin C). My work translated technical product information — ingredient, concern, routine position, timing — into communication a customer could actually act on, without burying it in ingredient terminology or pushing every product at once.

The challenge

A customer might know they want "better skin" without knowing which concern to prioritize, whether a product suits sensitive skin, or where it belongs in a routine. Product names themselves stack several types of information — "Copper Peptide Serum // GHK-Cu Firming + Barrier Hydration" — useful on a product page, too dense for a subject line. The real job wasn't promoting individual serums; it was building a decision path: concern → ingredient → product → routine position → usage guidance → replenishment.

The strategic decision

I treated AZORÉE LAB as a routine-building system, not a collection of unrelated serums. Central principle: one concern, one next step, one understandable routine. A dehydration-curious customer doesn't get a grid of every serum — they get what dehydration looks like, which category addresses it, where that product sits in the routine, and what reasonably comes next.

What I worked on
Campaign strategy and product/ingredient research
Back-in-stock campaign development
Product-benefit hierarchy and brand tone adaptation
Subscription-communication strategy
Shopify product verification and offer/price validation
Lifecycle-flow architecture and routine/replenishment recommendations
Cross-brand campaign adaptation
Campaign QA and mobile-first content structure
Tone-of-voice balance

The copy had to avoid two extremes: too vague ("Reveal your best skin ever" — no practical information) and too technical (naming six botanicals in a hero line). The working balance: "A gentler way to support smoother-looking skin", then one supporting sentence connecting the ingredient to the benefit. Lead with the concern, support with the ingredient, finish with the action.

Selected real campaign: Bakuchiol Serum back-in-stock

One product, one availability message, one CTA — deliberately no unrelated categories or competing offers. Sender: Only Blooms | Skincare. Subject: "Back in stock: AZORÉE LAB Bakuchiol Serum." Preview: "A gentle retinol alternative for smoother, more radiant-looking skin." This built a reusable back-in-stock format (hero → ingredient/concern education → routine position → service reassurance → exit on purchase) used again for other AZORÉE LAB restocks. The same product was also positioned inside OnlyBlooms' multi-brand system — there, the email had to establish the brand fast and compete with other categories, prioritizing recognition and one hero product over ingredient philosophy.

Product-education framework

Every campaign works through three levels: concern (dryness, dullness, sensitivity), ingredient mechanism (Hyaluronic Acid for hydration, Niacinamide for balance, Bakuchiol for gentler age support), and routine position (cleanser → toner → serum → moisturizer, AM or PM). This stops ingredients from being presented as isolated trends disconnected from where they actually go.

Automated flows
  • 01Welcome & concern discovery — "What does your skin need most right now?" determines the next email, not a generic product grid.
  • 02Concern & product browse recovery — a hydration viewer gets Hyaluronic Acid + moisturizer; an anti-aging viewer gets Bakuchiol + Copper Peptide, explained rather than dumped together.
  • 03First-purchase onboarding — drop count, AM/PM timing, and layering order, tailored to serum vs. cleanser vs. moisturizer.
  • 04Routine completion — cleanser → toner, serum → moisturizer — explains why the next step belongs, without implying everyone needs every product.
  • 05Replenishment & subscription conversion — timed to product size and usage; repeat buyers without a subscription get convenience framing (10% savings, pause/skip, gift every second delivery) not a restrictive-commitment pitch.
  • 06Back-in-stock & new-launch education — product-specific only; a new serum (e.g. Copper Peptide) is explained against existing ones rather than lumped in as "another anti-aging serum."

My delivered work centered on campaign strategy and the back-in-stock system; the wider flow architecture is a recommended lifecycle design, not a claim every journey has launched.

Segmentation
Skin type (dry / sensitive / mature / oily) Concern (hydration / firmness / brightening) Product affinity (Bakuchiol / HA / Niacinamide) Routine maturity Active subscriber Paused subscriber One-time purchaser At-risk / lapsed
Product claims & compliance
  • Use only claims already approved on product pages
  • Distinguish cosmetic appearance from medical outcomes
  • Avoid promising permanent or guaranteed results, or diagnosing skin conditions
  • Use "helps," "supports," "visibly," "appearance of" rather than absolute claims
  • Never present ingredient science as proof the finished product produced a specific clinical result
Communication-priority logic
  • A customer reporting a reaction doesn't get a routine-expansion promotion
  • A Bakuchiol purchaser exits its own back-in-stock sequence
  • An active subscriber doesn't get a one-time replenishment reminder for the same product
  • A paused subscriber gets account-management guidance before promotional pressure
Tools
Klaviyo Shopify HubSpot CRM Canva Figma Claude
Outcomes

A focused, reusable back-in-stock format; clearer separation between product benefit and ingredient detail; concise mobile-first copy; a lifecycle model connecting concerns, routines, replenishment, and subscriptions; and better coordination between the direct brand store and the multi-brand retail channel.

AZORÉE LAB is a real client project. The brand, product catalogue, Bakuchiol back-in-stock campaign, and cross-brand retail work described are real; public product, concern, and subscription details were checked against the live website. The wider lifecycle-flow section shows the strategic CRM architecture designed around the project — journeys not yet fully launched are labeled as designed systems, not measured implementations. I'm not including revenue, open-rate, click-rate, or subscription figures, as I only present results directly attributable and verifiable through client reporting.

Strategy Lab · Practice

Strategy Lab

Self-directed strategy exercises — fintech onboarding, product-led SaaS, and a Baltic marketplace — built to practice lifecycle thinking outside client work. Not real clients, and no real performance data.

Halyard Bank Hypothetical client Lifecycle strategy for a fintech onboarding and activation funnel Strategy sampleKlaviyoFintech 2026
Strategy sampleKlaviyoFintechLifecycle automationOmnichannel CRMAnalytics

A self-directed strategy exercise: how I'd architect a full lifecycle program for a digital bank, from account creation and identity verification through to a funded, transacting, retained customer. Halyard is fictional — built to show how I approach a new vertical, translate business problems into customer journeys, and connect messaging to measurable activation outcomes. A real financial-services build would go through legal, compliance, product, and security review before anything launches.

The brief

Halyard is a fictional neobank for freelancers and gig workers. Signups are strong, but a lot of users go quiet after registering — either they never finish verification, or they verify and never fund the account. The objective: move users from Registered → Verified → Funded → First transaction → Repeat use, without adding friction or breaking financial-services compliance, consent, or data-security requirements.

What I designed
Lifecycle stages and customer movement logic
Event and profile-property tracking
Behavioral segmentation
Email, SMS, push, in-app, and support journeys
Automated activation and retention flows
Campaign and educational-content planning
Suppression and consent logic
Experimentation priorities
Lifecycle dashboards and reporting
Compliance-aware deliverability practices
Lifecycle map

Signed up → Verification pending → Verified, not funded → Funding initiated → Funded, no transaction → Active user → At risk → Dormant → Churned. Every message triggers off a real behavior change, not a fixed send schedule — and a user exits a flow the moment they complete the action it's nudging them toward.

Data & event architecture
Core events
Account created Verification started Verification completed Verification failed Funding page viewed Funding initiated First deposit completed First transaction completed Feature used Monthly recap viewed Support request submitted 30 / 60 / 90 days inactive
Profile properties
Acquisition source Freelancer / gig segment Verification status Funding status First-transaction status Features adopted Last activity date Preferred channel Marketing consent Country & language Support / verification blocker Risk & suppression status
Channel strategy
EmailOnboarding education, trust-building, step-by-step guidance, monthly insights, reactivation.
SMSTime-sensitive reminders and verification deadlines, sent only with the required consent.
Push & in-appContextual prompts while the user is already in the product — mid-deposit, mid-feature.
Website & landing pagesPersonalized progress indicators and onboarding checklists reinforce the same next action.
Chat & supportUsers blocked by verification or funding go to support, not more promotional reminders.
Paid audience syncInactive segments sync to paid platforms for reactivation; active users are excluded.
Automated flows
  • 01Welcome & onboarding orientation — trigger: account created. One clear next action, not a feature dump.
  • 02Verification completion — trigger: verification incomplete. Timed reminders based on real drop-off points; exits the moment verification completes.
  • 03Verification issue & support — trigger: verification blocked or failed. Routes to support instead of more marketing reminders.
  • 04First-funding flow — trigger: verified, not funded. Step-by-step deposit guidance, trust signals, objection handling.
  • 05Funding-intent recovery — trigger: funding started, not completed. Addresses payment, trust, or technical drop-off.
  • 06First-transaction activation — trigger: funded, no transaction. Shows the simplest real use case, fast.
  • 07First-transaction moment — trigger: first transaction completed. A milestone message plus one relevant next feature.
  • 08Engagement & monthly insights — trigger: active account. An automated monthly recap built to create a habit loop.
  • 09At-risk & dormant reactivation — trigger: 30 / 60 / 90 days inactive. Message strength scales with how long they've been gone.
  • 10Compliance & consent — trigger: policy, consent, or security change. Kept fully separate from marketing sends.
Segmentation
Verification incomplete Verification failed Verified, never funded Funding abandoned Funded, never transacted Newly activated Low feature adoption Highly engaged 30-day inactive 60-day inactive 90-day inactive Opted out Restricted / suppressed
What I'd test
  • Message timing after each lifecycle event
  • Reminder count before escalating to support
  • Educational vs. benefit-led messaging
  • Single-step vs. checklist-based onboarding
  • Email-only vs. coordinated email + SMS journeys
  • Support-first vs. reminder-first recovery

Where possible, I'd run holdout groups to estimate incremental lift rather than assume every conversion after a send was caused by that send.

Compliance & deliverability
  • Separate infrastructure for transactional vs. marketing mail
  • Consent and preference-center management, GDPR-aware data handling
  • Suppression logic tied to consent and account status
  • Restricted use of sensitive financial data inside marketing platforms
  • SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication on dedicated sending domains
  • Subject-line and copy review to reduce spam risk
How I'd measure it
Activation
↑Verification completion rate
↑Verified → funded conversion
↓Time to first deposit
↓Time to first transaction
Retention
↑30 & 90-day active rate
↑Repeat-transaction rate
↑Dormant reactivation rate
~38%Automation revenue share, as a benchmark — this is what I reached at ZBL Agency
Channel & analysis
↑Conversion by flow step
↑Revenue / activation value per recipient
DirectionalOpen rate — a signal, not the main measure
A/B + holdoutCohort, funnel, and incremental-lift analysis

The numbers above are success criteria I'd define up front, not measured results — Halyard is a hypothetical company built to demonstrate the approach. In my real lifecycle work I've helped grow automation revenue share from under 20% to roughly 38%, built multi-flow automation systems from zero, and grown subscriber databases by 5,000+ profiles.

Kiteframe Hypothetical client Lifecycle strategy for a product-led SaaS activation and retention funnel Strategy sampleCustomer.ioSaaS 2026
Strategy sampleCustomer.ioHubSpot CRMSaaSProduct-led growthProduct analytics

A self-directed strategy exercise: how I'd architect a full lifecycle program for a B2B SaaS product, from account creation and workspace setup through activation, paid conversion, product adoption, and expansion. Kiteframe is fictional — built to show how I translate in-product behavior into lifecycle stages and connect CRM messaging to real usage rather than just opens and clicks. A real implementation would be developed with product, engineering, customer success, sales, and data-security teams.

The brief

Kiteframe is a fictional workflow automation platform with a 14-day free trial. Acquisition is strong, but many users sign up without ever reaching the moment they experience the product's real value — they stall before creating a workspace, installing a template, connecting an integration, or publishing and successfully running a workflow. The objective: move users through Signed up → Workspace created → First workflow published → First successful run → Activated → Paid → Retained → Expanded, reducing time to value without burying users in generic onboarding messages.

What I designed
Lifecycle stages and activation-based customer movement
Product-qualified lead scoring
Event and profile-property tracking
Behavioral segmentation
Email, in-app, push, sales, and CS journeys
Onboarding, conversion, adoption, and retention automation
Trial-expiration and payment-recovery logic
Suppression, consent, and message-priority rules
Experimentation priorities
Lifecycle dashboards and reporting
Lifecycle map

Anonymous visitor → Signed up → Workspace created → Setup incomplete → Workflow published → First successful run → Activated trial → Paid customer → Retained → Expansion-ready → At risk → Cancelled → Churned. Every journey triggers off an actual product-behavior change — a user who connects an integration right after a reminder exits that journey immediately and moves to the next relevant stage.

Data & event architecture
Core product events
Trial started Workspace created Use case selected Template installed Integration connected Integration connection failed Workflow published Workflow run completed Workflow run failed First successful workflow completed Teammate joined Subscription started Payment failed Subscription cancelled
Profile & account properties
Job function Company size Primary use case Trial end date Current plan Onboarding stage Activation score Product-qualified lead score Workflows published Integrations connected Teammates invited Customer-health score Sales-contact status Risk & suppression status
Channel strategy
EmailSetup guidance, use-case examples, trial reminders, adoption campaigns, value recaps.
In-app messagingContextual prompts tied to the exact page or feature the user is on right now.
Push notificationsMeaningful product events only — a workflow failed, ran successfully, or needs attention.
Sales-assisted outreachHigh-intent accounts with strong usage signals get routed to a rep, not another drip email.
Customer-success outreachTechnical blockers or high-value inactivity get a human, not more automation.
Paid audience syncInactive trials and churned accounts sync for controlled reactivation; active users are excluded.
Automated flows
  • 01Trial welcome & orientation — trigger: trial started. Helps the user find one relevant starting point instead of a feature tour.
  • 02Workspace-creation recovery — trigger: account created, no workspace. Escalates from a short nudge to asking what's blocking setup.
  • 03Use-case & template recommendation — trigger: workspace created, nothing installed. Content branches by job function.
  • 04Integration connection — trigger: template installed, no integration. Explains why it's needed and removes technical uncertainty.
  • 05Integration-error recovery — trigger: connection failed. Transactional, operational tone — routes to support after repeat failures.
  • 06First-workflow publishing — trigger: workflow created, not published. References the exact missing step.
  • 07First successful workflow run — trigger: published, no successful run. The single most important activation journey.
  • 08Activation milestone — trigger: first successful run completed. Confirms value and suggests one next action, not a feature list.
  • 09Trial-expiration & paid conversion — trigger: trial active, unpaid. Messaging branches by activation level, not a fixed countdown.
  • 10At-risk & cancellation prevention — trigger: declining health score or cancellation started. Asks the reason before offering a fix.
Segmentation
Signed up, no workspace Template viewed, not installed Integration failed Published, no successful run Activated trial High buying intent Checkout abandoned Paid, not fully activated Product-qualified lead Expansion-ready Fourteen-day inactive Cancellation intent
What I'd test
  • Blank-workspace vs. template-first onboarding
  • Product-led prompts vs. email-led prompts
  • Immediate vs. post-activation teammate invitation
  • Plan recommendation vs. generic pricing-page CTA
  • Sales outreach at different activation-score thresholds
  • Monthly recap vs. weekly recap cadence

Where possible, I'd run holdout groups to estimate incremental lift rather than assume every activation or upgrade after a message was caused by that message.

Compliance & deliverability
  • Separate transactional and marketing communication
  • GDPR-aware consent and preference-center management
  • No confidential workflow content inside email personalization
  • Immediate suppression after account deletion or opt-out
  • SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication on dedicated subdomains
  • Seed-list and rendering tests before every launch
How I'd measure it
Activation
↑Trial activation rate
↓Time to first successful workflow
↑Integration-connection rate
Conversion & retention
↑Trial-to-paid conversion, activated users
↓Checkout abandonment
↑Thirty-day active-account retention
↑Seat / plan expansion among eligible accounts

The numbers above are success criteria I'd define up front, not measured results — Kiteframe is a hypothetical company built to demonstrate my approach to SaaS lifecycle strategy. In my real lifecycle work I've built multi-flow automation systems from zero, helped grow automation revenue share from under 20% to roughly 38%, and grown subscriber databases by 5,000+ profiles.

Baltora Market Hypothetical client Lifecycle strategy for a Baltic e-commerce marketplace Strategy sampleKlaviyoMarketplace 2026
Strategy sampleKlaviyoE-commerceMarketplaceLocalizationAnalytics

A self-directed strategy exercise: how I'd architect a full lifecycle program for a multi-vendor marketplace operating across Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia. Baltora Market is fictional — built to show how I translate buyer and seller behavior into automated journeys, localize communication across Baltic markets, and connect CRM activity to measurable retention and marketplace growth. A real build would be developed with e-commerce, product, logistics, vendor management, and legal teams.

The brief

Baltora Market sells beauty, wellness, home, food, and gift products from independent regional sellers across three Baltic markets. Traffic and first orders are growing, but customers often browse without subscribing, buy once and don't return, or abandon multi-seller baskets when delivery conditions are unclear — while sellers join but stall before completing a catalogue or landing a first sale. The goal: move buyers through Visitor → Subscriber → First-time buyer → Repeat buyer → Loyal customer, and sellers through Registered → Catalogue completed → First order → High-performing seller — without leaning on discounts as the default lever.

What I designed
Connected buyer and seller lifecycle stages
Customer identity resolution across guest, account, and app activity
Country and language localization logic
Product and category affinity segmentation
Browse, cart, checkout, and search recovery
Post-purchase, replenishment, and loyalty journeys
Seller onboarding and performance journeys
Discount-control and suppression logic
Experimentation priorities
Marketplace dashboards and reporting
Lifecycle map

Buyer: Anonymous visitor → Subscriber → Product explorer → Cart creator → First-time buyer → Repeat buyer → Loyal customer → VIP → At risk → Lapsed. Seller: Registered → Approved → Catalogue completed → Products published → First order → Active seller → High-performing seller. A shopper exits cart recovery the moment they check out; a seller exits basic onboarding the moment their catalogue is live.

Data & event architecture
Buyer events
Newsletter subscribed Product viewed Search returned no results Back-in-stock request submitted Multi-seller cart created Checkout started Payment failed First order completed Order delivered Delivery delayed Loyalty reward earned Ninety days inactive
Seller events
Seller approved Store profile completed Catalogue completed Product rejected First seller order received Order delayed Payout completed Seller review received Seller performance threshold reached Seller inactive for thirty days Stock coverage decreased
Channel strategy
EmailWelcome, discovery, cart/checkout recovery, post-purchase, replenishment, seller onboarding.
SMSDelivery updates and parcel-locker deadlines only — not a repeat of every email.
PushBack-in-stock, price drops, wishlist updates, delivery status, loyalty rewards.
Website personalizationRecently viewed, country-specific recommendations, replenishment suggestions.
Customer supportUnresolved delivery or seller disputes route to service, not promotional sends.
Paid audience syncLapsed and high-intent non-buyers sync out; recent purchasers and open cases are excluded.
Automated flows
  • 01Welcome & preference capture — trigger: subscription. Captures category and country before pushing every product on the site.
  • 02First-purchase conversion — trigger: subscriber, no order. Content branches on browse behavior rather than defaulting to a discount.
  • 03Browse & search recovery — trigger: repeated views or a zero-result search. Also feeds unmet-demand data back to merchandising.
  • 04Multi-seller cart education — trigger: cart spans sellers. Explains split shipping and returns before checkout, not after a complaint.
  • 05Cart & checkout recovery — trigger: abandoned cart or checkout. Payment failures route to operational recovery, not marketing.
  • 06First-order education — trigger: first order placed. Explains split parcels and tracking before trying to sell again.
  • 07Delivery & delay recovery — trigger: delayed shipment. Suppresses promotional sends until the issue resolves.
  • 08Second-purchase & replenishment — trigger: delivered, no repeat order. The single most important retention milestone.
  • 09Seller onboarding & activation — trigger: approved seller, incomplete catalogue or no first sale. Checklist-based, not a generic newsletter.
  • 10Seller-risk recovery — trigger: declining seller-health score. Operational issues escalate to vendor management first.
Segmentation
Subscribers without purchases Multi-seller cart users Checkout abandoners Lithuanian / Latvian / Estonian customers Discount-sensitive buyers High-LTV customers Replenishment-due customers Open delivery issue Store incomplete sellers High-performing sellers Inactive sellers
What I'd test
  • Category-selection onboarding vs. general welcome
  • Free delivery vs. percentage discount as the first-purchase lever
  • Product-led vs. trust-led cart recovery copy
  • Country-specific vs. Baltic-wide recommendations
  • Checklist-based vs. guided seller onboarding
  • Loyalty reward vs. free-delivery reward for retention

Where possible, I'd run holdout groups to estimate incremental lift rather than assume every order after a message was caused by that message.

Compliance & deliverability
  • Separate transactional and marketing communication
  • Country and language preference management, GDPR-aware
  • No full payment details inside marketing platforms
  • Immediate opt-out and suppression updates
  • SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication on dedicated domains
  • Product price, stock, and country-availability validation before send
How I'd measure it
Buyer activation
↑Subscriber-to-first-purchase conversion
↓Checkout abandonment
↑First-to-second-order conversion
Experience & sellers
↓Support contacts from multi-seller delivery confusion
↓Time from seller approval to first published product
↑Approved sellers reaching their first order
↑Active seller rate

The numbers above are success criteria I'd define up front, not measured results — Baltora Market is a hypothetical company built to demonstrate my approach to Baltic e-commerce marketplace strategy. In my real lifecycle work I've built multi-flow automation systems from zero, worked with e-commerce catalogues and promotional campaigns, helped grow automation revenue share from under 20% to roughly 38%, and grown subscriber databases by 5,000+ profiles.

Collaboration & project management

How I actually coordinate people, tasks, deadlines, approvals and reporting — connected to a real process, not a logo cloud.

Workflow · how-the-work-moves Operating stack
SlackCommunication — priorities, questions, blockers
ClickUpTask — owner, deadline, priority, subtasks
BriefObjectives, segmentation, copy, design references, assets
ProductionCopy, design and technical build coordination
QAAudience, content, links, tracking, rendering, offer
ApprovalDocumented, launch-ready confirmation
LaunchCampaign scheduled or flow activated
ReportingExcel / Sheets dashboard + next actions

Slack keeps communication fast; ClickUp stays the source of truth for ownership, deadlines, files and approval status.

ClickUp · task-structure Task structure

Task title format: Brand | Campaign name | Planned send date

BrandCampaign typeBusiness objective OwnerPriorityDeadline Lifecycle stageAudienceCurrent status Approval statusLaunch dateReporting status
  • 01Confirm brief and offer
  • 02Create audience segment
  • 03Write subject line and preview text
  • 04Prepare campaign copy
  • 05Create or request design
  • 06Build the campaign
  • 07Complete QA
  • 08Receive approval
  • 09Schedule or launch
  • 10Add results to the dashboard
  • 11Document learning and next steps
Brief neededIn planningCopy in progress Design in progressReady to buildIn QA Waiting for approvalScheduledSent Reporting complete
Slack communication
Daily prioritiesWhat's launching today, what needs approval, and what's blocked.
Campaign feedbackCopy, design and technical comments linked to the relevant ClickUp task.
Urgent updatesChanges to prices, stock, offers, deadlines or customer communication.
Cross-functional coordinationQuestions for design, development, support, product and management.
Post-launch updatesDelivery issues, early campaign signals, and actions requiring attention.

"Slack supported fast communication, while ClickUp remained the source of truth for ownership, deadlines, files, approvals and task status."

Reporting and efficiency dashboards

I used Excel and Google Sheets for both customer-support and lifecycle-marketing operations — tracked as ongoing indicators, not one-off reports. Includes pivot tables, XLOOKUP/VLOOKUP, SUMIF/COUNTIF, conditional formatting, data validation, structured tables and chart-based summaries.

Excel · support-dashboard Indicators tracked
Request volumeOpen & overdue cases SLA complianceFirst-response time Resolution timeQA score Workload by team memberEscalations Customer issue categoriesDaily / weekly / monthly productivity Efficiency trends
Sheets · lifecycle-marketing-dashboard Indicators tracked
Campaign name & brandCampaign objective Send dateAudience sizeDelivery rate Open rateClick-through rateConversion rate Revenue per recipientUnsubscribe rate Coupon-redemption rateFlow performance Abandoned-cart recoveryAutomation revenue share Campaign statusOwnerProduction time Revision roundsQA issuesLearning & next action

These are indicators I tracked, not standalone claims. Verified results from this system — automation open rate, click-through rate, cart-recovery rate and coupon redemption — are shown in the OnlyBlooms case study above.

Dashboard purpose: My dashboards were management tools, not static reports. I used them to identify bottlenecks, compare performance, balance workloads, maintain deadlines, monitor team efficiency, and decide what should be improved next.

Project-management principles
One source of truth — tasks, deadlines, ownership and approvals live in ClickUp, not scattered across messages.
Fast communication, structured execution — Slack supports quick decisions; ClickUp records the final requirements and status.
Visible workload — Excel, Sheets and ClickUp dashboards make team capacity and active priorities visible.
Standardized checklists — recurring ClickUp tasks and checklists reduce missed steps across support and email-marketing operations.
Data-informed management — KPI dashboards help identify performance changes, operational bottlenecks and improvement opportunities.
Documented learning — campaign results, customer feedback and process issues are recorded and used to improve future work.
Cross-functional tool map
Marketing & CRM teamSlack, ClickUp, Excel and Google Sheets.
Design teamClickUp, Slack, Figma and Canva.
Development & technical supportClickUp, Slack, technical documentation, and Jira-style task structures where relevant.
Customer-support teamSlack, ClickUp, knowledge base, daily checklist and KPI dashboard.
ManagementExcel or Google Sheets performance summaries, ClickUp progress visibility, and regular reporting.
Sent · Experience

How customer insight became lifecycle strategy

My career started with understanding people and solving customer problems. Each role added a new layer: retention, operations, CRM, automation, revenue, and leadership.

Soft Skills Teacher
GoITeens IT Academy · Kyiv, Remote

Where the funnel led me

Lifecycle Marketing Manager

Today, I connect customer behavior, CRM infrastructure, creative communication, automation, and analytics to build lifecycle systems that increase retention and revenue.

Customer conversations→ Retention insight→ CRM systems→ Automated journeys→ Revenue growth

The person behind the lifecycle

Draft · profile.html Saved
Blocks
Header
Text
Stats
List
Button

I build the strategy, systems, and processes behind customer retention.

My work sits between CRM strategy and hands-on execution. I map customer journeys, design segmentation logic, build automated flows, develop testing frameworks, and turn performance data into clear decisions for the business.

I have worked across e-commerce categories including floristry, specialty coffee, wellness, beauty, and gifting. This variety has taught me how to adapt lifecycle programs to different customer behaviours, purchase cycles, and commercial goals.

My earlier experience in customer support, retention operations, and B2B outreach continues to shape how I approach CRM. I think beyond the message itself, considering the data, workflows, team coordination, and customer experience required to make every campaign work.
